title: Measuring Domain Shift
concept: Track out-of-vocabulary rates and perplexity on the target domain to measure how much the model's pretraining deviates from the deployment task.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need to measure domain shift before deploying an AI model?

options.text: To check how much the real-world data differs from the training data
To make the model run faster on mobile phones
To delete old data from the database

exercises.explain: Measuring domain shift helps us understand if the text our model is seeing in the real world is too different from the data it was trained on, which could cause it to fail.

cards.title: Lost in Translation?
cards.body: AI models are like language travelers. If they learn English by reading classic novels and suddenly get dropped into a high-tech coding forum, they will get lost in translation. But how do we actually measure how lost they are? Let's find out!

cards.title: What is Domain Shift?
cards.body: Domain Shift happens when the data your model was trained on is different from the data it meets in the real world. To measure this gap, we track two key metrics: Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) rate (how many words are completely new to the model) and Perplexity (how surprised or confused the model is by the new sentences).

cards.title: The Guide Dog in a New City
cards.body: Imagine you bought a guide dog trained exclusively on the quiet streets of a small town, and then you moved to the middle of Tokyo. The dog will be confused by all the new signs, flashing lights, and crowds. Measuring domain shift is like counting how many unfamiliar sights and sounds the dog encounters in Tokyo compared to its quiet hometown to see if it needs extra training.

cards.title: Wikipedia vs. Medical Records
cards.body: If you train a model on Wikipedia articles (General Domain) and then test it on Medical Records (Target Domain), you might find that 12% of the medical words are completely missing from its vocabulary (high OOV rate), and its confusion level (perplexity) jumps from 15 to 150. This tells you the model is struggling to understand the new environment.

cards.title: Myth: Smart Models Don't Get Confused
cards.body: People often think that if a model is smart, it will automatically handle any new topic. In reality, even the most advanced models get confused when the vocabulary and sentence structures change. Measuring domain shift is a requirement, not an option!

cards.title: Visualizing the Shift
cards.body: By looking at the vocabulary mismatch and the model's confusion level, we can visualize the distance between domains.

left.text: Low OOV rate (few new words) & Low perplexity (low confusion). The model feels right at home!

right.text: High OOV rate (many strange words) & High perplexity (high confusion). The model is completely lost.

exercises.prompt: What does the Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) rate measure?

options.text: The percentage of words in the new data that the model has never seen before.
The speed at which the model processes new words.
The number of grammatical errors the model makes.
The total size of the model's memory footprint.

exercises.explain: The Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) rate tells us the percentage of words in the new dataset that the model has never seen before in its training vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a model has a very low perplexity on a new target dataset, it means the domain shift is extremely high.
exercises.explain: High perplexity means the model is highly confused by the new data, which indicates a large domain shift.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence explaining model confusion.
exercises.sentence: When a model meets a new domain, we measure its confusion or surprise using a metric called [blank].
exercises.explain: Perplexity measures how surprised or confused a model is when trying to predict the next word in a sentence. A high perplexity indicates a large domain shift.

exercises.prompt: A general-purpose AI model is evaluated on a medical dataset. Which of these outputs shows the clearest signs of domain shift confusion?

options.text: The patient presents with acute myocardial infarction symptoms.
The human has a chest pain thingy and needs the body repair shop.

exercises.explain: Only Option B represents an AI-generated output from a model experiencing high domain shift, as it fails to understand specialized medical terminology ("heart attack") and defaults to common daily words.

exercises.prompt: You are preparing to deploy a customer support assistant to a medical clinic. Which combination of metrics would warn you that the medical domain is too different from the model's original general training data, requiring you to adapt the model?

options.text: High OOV rate and high perplexity
Low OOV rate and low perplexity
Zero OOV rate and low perplexity
High training accuracy and low validation loss

exercises.explain: To successfully deploy a model to a medical domain, you must track indicators like Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) rate and Perplexity. If these numbers are high, it means the model is encountering too many unfamiliar terms and struggles to predict the next word, signalling a strong domain shift.
